gloss: to raise a solid to vapour
definition: To *sublimate* is to heat a solid until it turns straight to vapor, skipping the liquid state, then let it recondense as a purified solid higher in the vessel — a rising and refining in one motion. It differs from *distillation*, which passes through a liquid and drips down; sublimation lifts a dry substance up. Alchemists sublimated mercury, sulphur, and arsenic to separate their 'spirits' from their gross bodies.
history: Latin *sublimare*, 'to raise up' (from *sublimis*, 'lofty'), fit an operation that literally elevates matter — Zosimos used fire in distillation, sublimation, and volatilization to separate the spirits from the bodies. The turn is a borrowing outward. Renaissance Neoplatonists already spoke of a sublimation of the soul and of desire spiritualized; Freud then took the word for psychic life, naming *sublimation* the redirecting of instinct from a base to a noble object. So the alembic's rising vapor became the analyst's model for desire raised toward higher ends — the operation carried, unbroken, from furnace to couch.

def_sources.label: Hanegraaff, Hermetic Spirituality and the Historical Imagination (on Zosimos)
def_sources.abstract: Looks at the ancient Egyptian writings attributed to the legendary teacher Hermes Trismegistus and argues they weren't just abstract philosophy — they describe real spiritual exercises meant to shift a person's state of mind so they could see past ordinary illusions to a deeper truth about reality.

commentary: The alchemical operations invite a special version of the alchemy debate. In the wake of Carl Jung and Mircea Eliade, *sublimatio*, *putrefactio*, and the rest were read as coded stages of the soul's transformation — an inner drama in chemical dress. Lawrence Principe and William Newman have insisted instead that these were real laboratory procedures: Zosimos used fire to separate spirits from bodies because he was actually subliming solids. Newman's study of Thomas Vaughan shows the period's own authors distinguishing operations on *physical substance* from any *unio mystica*, and Wouter Hanegraaff traces the purely 'spiritual' reading to a modern, and in his phrase 'degenerate,' construction.

commentary_sources.label: Lawrence Principe & William Newman, 'Some Problems with the Historiography of Alchemy'
commentary_sources.abstract: A widely cited essay (a chapter in the book Secrets of Nature) arguing that many 20th-century scholars misread old alchemy as secret spiritual symbolism, when most alchemists were actually doing hands-on laboratory work aimed at real, physical transformations of matter.

commentary_sources.label: William Newman, 'Thomas Vaughan as an Interpreter of Agrippa' (Ambix, 1982)
commentary_sources.abstract: Examines how the 17th-century alchemist Thomas Vaughan read and used the earlier writer Agrippa's ideas, arguing Vaughan understood alchemy as involving real physical processes rather than purely spiritual metaphor.

commentary_sources.label: Wouter J. Hanegraaff, Esotericism and the Academy
commentary_sources.abstract: Explains why universities have historically dismissed astrology, alchemy, and magic as "not real knowledge," and argues that this rejection says as much about how scholars built their own identity as it does about the actual content of those traditions.

etymology.note: Solid straight to vapour, skipping liquid — a rising. Freud borrowed the word for desire redirected upward.
etymology.hook: From the alembic to the analyst's couch.

flavor_snippets.html: "Specimen 211 examination report. Observation: graft <mark>sublimation</mark> incomplete. Result: death. Prepare specimen 212."
